---
title: WIP- Climate change will cook chestnut-breasted whiteface
author:
  - name: Ned L Ryan-Schofield
    orcid: 0000-0002-4997-6560
    corresponding: true
    email: ned.ryan-schofield@bushheritage.org.au
    roles:
      - Conceptualisation
      - Data Collection
      - Analysis
      - Manuscript preparation
    affiliations:
      - Bush Heritage Australia
      - Charles Darwin University
  - name: Darcy A Whittaker
    orcid:  
    corresponding: false
    roles:  
      - Conceptualisation
      - Data Collection
      - Manuscript preparation
    affiliations:
      - Bush Heritage Australia
  - name: Graeme R Finlayson
    orcid: 0000-0002-6770-2520 
    corresponding: false
    roles:  
      - Conceptualisation
      - Manuscript preparation
    affiliations:
      - Bush Heritage Australia
  - name: Lucy McCormack
    orcid:  
    corresponding: false
    roles:  
      - Analysis
      - Manuscript preparation
    affiliations:
      - Bush Heritage Australia
  - name: Patrick L Taggart
    orcid: 0000-0001-9523-0463 
    corresponding: false
    roles:  
      - Conceptualisation
      - Data Collection
      - Analysis
      - Manuscript preparation
    affiliations:
      - Bush Heritage Australia
keywords:
  - Climate change
  - Heatwaves
abstract: |
  tba ...
plain-language-summary: |
  tba ...
key-points:
  - tba
date: last-modified
bibliography: references.bib
citation:
  container-title: Earth and Space Science
number-sections: true
---

NRS NOTES 28/5/26:
- Figures are produced by the scripts in https://github.com/nedschofield/cbwf_-manuscript_project.git, stored locally, and just called from this manuscript.
- After I finalise the analysis I'll port it all over into notebooks/ so that it is all reproducible, but for now I want to keep just one version of the analysis.


## Introduction

With current and ongoing high rates of extinction, the importance of ecological monitoring is fundamental to understanding the natural environment and conserving species [@lindenmayer2018]. Identifying declining populations through effective biodiversity monitoring can prove critical in implementing timely conservation measures [@baker2019][@butchart2006]. This is particularly pertinent for threatened, rare and range-restricted species. Such species are at greater risk of rapid and unrecognised decline due to their increased susceptibility to stochastic events [@smith2020].  

Relative to other land types, Australian arid landscapes are typically characterised by low human population densities. A marked spatial unevenness in biodiversity data is seen across Australia, with sparsely populated areas often lacking in biological records [@woinarski2021]. In theory, these factors could contribute to species declines going unnoticed in remote areas [@woinarski2021]. Arid lands contain unique biodiversity components, the loss of which can have profound impacts on the overall ecosystem [@zhang2023]. Threatened species in such environments therefore require carefully designed monitoring programs to inform conservation management and better detect population changes.  

Arid and semi-arid rangelands cover a significant proportion of Australia, and like elsewhere they are characterised by low human population density, high numbers of threatened species and high rates of extinctions [@morton1995][@woinarski2003]. Since European settlement in Australia, 20 arid land species are known to have become extinct, with numerous others currently threatened with extinction [@woinarski2019]. The arid rangelands have experienced rapid and extensive change in the time since European colonisation. While significant swathes of land still fall under Indigenous management [@renwick2017], pastoralism is one of the major land uses in the Australian arid rangelands [@letnic2006]. Pastoral activities are known to have direct and indirect impacts on arid land ecosystem function [@letnic2007]. Along with the impacts of pastoralism, other threats pose major challenges to species living in the arid rangelands, such as introduced species and climate change [@garnett2024]. Naturally boom and bust systems, arid lands experience large fluctuations in available resources and environmental conditions over time [@letnic2006]. Species living in arid environments are adapted to these changing conditions, but survival under future climate change is likely to become increasingly challenging [@conradie2020]. By the end of the 21st century, Zebra Finches (*Taeniopygia castanotis*), a small arid-adapted passerine, are expected in some regions to experience a more than threefold increase in the number of days per year that they are exposed to lethal dehydration risk [@conradie2020]. 

The Chestnut-breasted Whiteface (*Aphelocephala pectoralis*) is a small passerine of arid, stony, low shrublands in northern South Australia. A South Australian endemic, *A. pectoralis* has a restricted range and has anecdotally faced declines in some areas. Prior to 1990, the species was known from only 33 localities (5 unconfirmed) [@pedler1992]. Proving elusive through the late 19th and mid-20th century, A. pectoralis went undetected for long periods of time on more than one occasion (43 years between the type specimen’s discovery in 1871 and the next recorded sighting in 1914, and again for another 43 years between 1923 and 1966) [@pedler1992]. In 1990, Lynn Pedler revisited almost all the then known A. pectoralis localities and also discovered several new occupied sites. Using the results from these surveys, and observed population densities from a study site in the eastern part of the species’ range (Mount Lyndhurst), an overall population of 6000 individuals was estimated (exact methodology not stated) [@pedler1992]. Some populations were again resurveyed in 1999 [@pedler2000] and the Mount Lyndhurst population monitored again in 2007 [@pedler2007].  

Despite the species’ apparent rarity, restricted range and anecdotal evidence of declines in some areas, A. pectoralis has had little to no research or conservation attention excluding the work of Pedler et al. Listed nationally as Near Threatened and as Rare in South Australia, A. pectoralis lacks contemporary abundance and distribution data and its conservation status may reflect this uncertainty [@garnett2021].  

This project aimed to reassess the abundance and distribution of the Chestnut-breasted Whiteface and help inform future conservation status assessments. To achieve this, as many of the original 1990 survey sites as possible were revisited in addition to a selection of sites where the species had since been detected. Through this process, we also aimed to develop a standardised, repeatable survey protocol suitable for A. pectoralis. Simultaneously, we aimed to collect as much biologically relevant information on the species as possible to help to understand their ecology and inform their conservation.

## Methods {#sec-data-methods}

### Survey Region and Site Selection

```{r}
#packages
library(dplyr)

#create a path to cbwf_project location
proj.path <- "C:/Users/nedsc/Documents/git/cbwf_project/"

#survey data
d <- read.csv(file = paste0(proj.path, "data/table/CBWF_surveys_11052026.csv"))

n_sites <- length(unique(d$site))
tot_cbwf <- d |> 
  group_by(site) |>
  summarise(tot = sum(CBWF))
sites_w_cbwf <- n_sites - nrow(subset(tot_cbwf, tot > 0))

```

Surveys were conducted across the known and suspected range of the Chestnut-breasted Whiteface *Aphelocephala pectoralis* in arid northern South Australia between June and September 2024. Survey sites were selected to reassess historical localities and more recent records of the species, rather than to provide a random sample of available habitat. The core set of survey sites was based on localities surveyed in [@pedler1992] during a review of the species’ distribution and status, including sites where Chestnut-breasted Whitefaces were detected and sites where surveys were unsuccessful. Additional sites were included from subsequent surveys in 1999–2000 [@pedler2000] and from more recent species records, particularly where these records represented areas not well covered by the 1990 survey network.

In total, `r n_sites` sites were surveyed. These sites were distributed across four broad regions: the Coober Pedy–Glendambo region; the Oodnadatta region, including sites between Marla and Oodnadatta and between Nilpinna and William Creek; the Marree–Leigh Creek region, including the Lyndhurst–Birdsville Track area; and the Gawler Ranges–Lake Gairdner region, including sites around Mt Ive and Nonning. Most sites occurred within arid stony plains, chenopod shrublands and associated low shrubland habitats on working pastoral properties.

Where accurate location information was available, survey cells were positioned to encompass the original historical survey locality as closely as possible. For some historical records, however, location information was imprecise or unavailable at the scale required to relocate the original survey point. In these cases, surveys were placed within the approximate locality described by the historical record and in habitat judged to be broadly comparable to that originally surveyed. A map of surveyed sites and their broad regional grouping is provided in @fig-cbwf-map.

### Survey Methods

At each site, we conducted a set of repeated area searches designed to provide standardised count data while maximising the probability of detecting a rare and potentially patchily distributed species. The standard survey unit was a 20-minute, 5-ha area search. Each site comprised ten such survey cells, giving a total surveyed area of 50 ha per site. Cells were arranged in two spatially separated transects, with five 5-ha cells in each transect. Transects were separated by 200m to reduce the risk of double-counting individuals and to minimise interference between observers and playback in adjacent transects. Each cell was surveyed once during a site visit.

Surveys were conducted on foot by a single observer per cell using binoculars. Where two observers were conducting the survey, observers surveyed separate transects independently, beginning at one end of the transect and moving sequentially through the five cells. This ensured a consistent direction of travel through each transect and reduced the likelihood of repeatedly flushing or re-counting birds between cells. In each cell, observers recorded the number of Chestnut-breasted Whiteface detected during the 20-minute search. For each Chestnut-breasted Whiteface detection, observers recorded the number of individuals detected and whether the initial detection occurred before or after call playback. Call playback was used to increase detectability. At the centre of each 5-ha cell, observers broadcast Chestnut-breasted Whiteface vocalisations consisting of 30 seconds of calls and song, followed by a 30-second listening period, then a second 30-second broadcast followed by a second 30-second listening period. The use of short, standardised playback bouts at the centre of each cell was intended to increase the probability of detecting birds present within the cell while limiting attraction from beyond the surveyed area. Playback was conducted consistently across cells so that survey effort was comparable among sites.

Surveys were generally conducted as early as practicable in the day, although the remoteness of sites and long travel distances meant that some surveys were completed later in the day. Surveys were not conducted, where avoidable, during very strong winds or other conditions likely to substantially reduce detectability. For each cell, observers recorded the date, survey start time and broad survey conditions, including wind, cloud cover, rain and temperature category. At the centre of each cell, four habitat photographs were taken facing approximately north, east, south and west. These photographs, together with field notes, were used to classify the broad habitat of each survey cell.

The ten-cell design provided replicated surveys within each site, increasing the probability of detecting Chestnut-breasted Whitefaces where present and providing repeated count data while accounting for imperfect detection. This design also balanced statistical and logistical requirements: greater replication was desirable because the species is rare and often occurs at low density, but survey effort had to remain feasible across a large, remote study region with widely separated sites.

### Data Analysis

#### Occupancy

We fitted a Bayesian single-season occupancy model in JAGS to estimate site-level occupancy probability (ψ) and detection probability (*p*). The global model included four site-level covariates:

- 6-month pre-survey rainfall,
- change in annual rainfall over the last 30 years,
- change in number of extreme heat days (days per year exceeding 40°C) over the last 30 years,
- and change in photosynthetic vegetation (PV) fractional cover over the last 30 years.

Model fit was assessed via Bayesian posterior predictive checks (Freeman-Tukey statistic) and convergence was confirmed using the Gelman-Rubin diagnostic (R̂ \< 1.1 for all parameters).

Occupancy probability was predicted spatially across the study region by applying the fitted model to 1 km² aggregated raster layers of each covariate. The September 6-month rainfall layer was used for spatial prediction, as the majority of surveys were conducted in September. Uncertainty was propagated by drawing 500 thinned samples from the joint posterior to produce 95% credible interval surfaces alongside the posterior mean.

#### Distribution modelling

To place the 2024 survey results in a historical and future climate context, we fitted a boosted regression tree (BRT) model of CBWF occurrence using survey records spanning 1960, 1990 and 2024 periods. We used 1960 as a centre for historical records that Lyn based his surveys off, as many of the historical records were from around that time, with some exceptions. we coerced our and Lyn's survey data into a presence/absence dataset, and added historic presence records of CBWF recorded in Lyn's paper. We then extracted climate data for each period to create a dataset suitable for predicting occurrence probability with boosted regression trees.

The model used two climate predictors: mean annual days with maximum temperature exceeding 40°C, and mean annual rainfall (mm), both derived from AGCD gridded climate data for each historical period.

To project occurence probability under future climate scenarios, we used NARCliM2.0 regional climate model output. Daily maximum temperature and precipitation data were downloaded for five global climate models (GCMs), three emissions scenarios (SSP1-2.6, SSP2-4.5, SSP3-7.0), and two regional climate model configurations (R3, R5), for two 20-year periods: mid-century (2040–2059) and late-century (2080–2099). For each GCM/SSP/RCM/period combination, we computed mean annual days over 40°C and mean annual rainfall, then ensemble-averaged across GCMs and RCMs within each SSP×period group, producing six climate scenarios for prediction.

## Results
```{r}
#| label: fig-cbwf-map
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "2024 Chestnut-breasted Whiteface survey detections"

knitr::include_graphics(
  file.path(proj.path, "figures", "updated_figs", "CBWF_survey_outcomes.png")
)
```

A total of `r sum(tot_cbwf$tot)` Chestnut-breasted Whiteface were detected from `r sites_w_cbwf` of `r n_sites` sites (mean = `r mean(tot_cbwf$tot)`, sd = `r sd(tot_cbwf$tot)`). Birds were only detected at sites in the Coober Pedy-Glendambo region @fig-cbwf-map <!-- NRS: this map needs improving but will do for draft -->.

### occupancy
```{r}
#| label: load-occu-data
occu_global <- readRDS(paste0(proj.path, "outputs/occu_global.rds"))
occu_null   <- readRDS(paste0(proj.path, "outputs/occu_null.rds"))
```

Detection probability was estimated to be *p* = `r round(occu_global$mean$p, 2)` (95% CrI: `r round(occu_global$q2.5$p, 2)`–`r round(occu_global$q97.5$p, 2)`), indicating moderate detectability per survey cell. Of the `r n_sites` sites surveyed, an estimated `r round(occu_global$mean$nocc, 1)` (95% CrI: `r round(occu_global$q2.5$nocc, 0)`–`r round(occu_global$q97.5$nocc, 0)`) were inferred to be occupied, slightly less than the observed number of sites where birds were recorded (`r sites_w_cbwf`). 

Covariate effects on occupancy are shown in @fig-coeffs, which indicates that change in number of extreme heat days over the past 30 years was the only informative predictor, with all other predictors credible intervals crossing zero. 

```{r}
#| label: fig-coeffs
#| fig-cap: "Posterior mean occupancy covariate effects with 95% credible intervals (logit scale). Points to the right of zero indicate a positive effect on occupancy probability."
#| out-width: 70%

knitr::include_graphics(paste0(proj.path, "figures/updated_figs/occu_global_covar_coeffs.png"))
```

Occupancy probability was predicted spatially across the study region by applying the fitted model to 1 km² aggregated raster layers of each covariate. The September 6-month rainfall layer was used for spatial prediction, as the majority of surveys were conducted in September. Uncertainty was propagated by drawing 500 thinned samples from the joint posterior to produce 95% credible interval surfaces alongside the posterior mean.

@fig-psi-maps shows areas with highest probability of occupancy are in the South-west part of the birds range, centering around Bon Bon and Glendambo. Parts of the range where no whiteface were detected during these surveys have much lower occupancy probability.

```{r}
#| label: fig-psi-maps
#| fig-cap: "Spatial prediction of occupancy probability ψ: posterior mean (left), lower 95% credible interval (centre), and upper 95% credible interval (right). Survey sites are shown as black points."
#| out-width: 100%
knitr::include_graphics(paste0(proj.path, "figures/updated_figs/occupancy_psi_maps.png"))
```

### Distribution modelling
The BRT model predicts occurrence probability across the historical study region for each of the three survey periods. Higher probability (warmer colours) reflects climate conditions more favourable to the species.In @fig-brt-hist, it is clear that the climate envelope suitable for CBWF has shrunk, as the climate has warmed over the last 60 years.

```{r}
#| label: fig-brt-hist
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Predicted occupancy surface."

knitr::include_graphics(
  file.path(proj.path, "figures", "updated_figs", "brt_historical_occupancy_sat.png")
)
```
Projected occupancy probability under NARCliM2.0 ensemble climate scenarios is shown in @fig-brt-fut. The model predicts significant declines in occurrence at currently known CBWF locations, across all emissions scenarios. By late century, most of the historical distribution of CBWF species is projected to be lost, under all emissions scenarios.

```{r}
#| label: fig-brt-fut
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Predicted occupancy surface."

knitr::include_graphics(
  file.path(proj.path, "figures", "updated_figs", "brt_narclim_occupancy_sat.png")
)
```

## Discussion
Interestingly, there is some parts of the predicted range to the north (around Indulkana in the APY lands) which we didn't actually survey that have high probability of occupancy. Birds are frequently recorded here by bellbird tours and other birders heading up the highway.
## References

::: {#refs}
:::
